brief:
The single most important signal this week is the complete absence of movement — a yield curve frozen in place across every tenor, with zero basis point change from 91-day bills through 20-year bonds, even as the government quietly swallowed a **10% devolvement on the 10-year BGTB** at the March 8 auction. That devolvement, combined with a thin **1.4x bid-cover** on the same instrument, tells us that the market is not rejecting government paper outright but is drawing a hard line on duration risk at current pricing. Compare that with the front end: the 91-day bill cleared at **9.85%** with a healthy **1.92x bid-cover** and zero devolvement, and the 364-day at **10.07%** with **1.75x coverage** — both comfortably absorbed. The short end is well-bid; the long end is on life support. What makes the curve truly unusual, however, is not the 10-year stress but the grotesque kink at the belly: the 2-year is printing at **11.95%**, a full **113 basis points above the 5-year** at 10.82% and **80 basis points over the 10-year** at 11.15%. Meanwhile, the 15-year sits at **12.68%**, overshooting the 20-year at 11.65% by over a hundred basis points. This is not a normal curve — it is a curve with two humps and an identity crisis, and it is telling us something important about where fiscal stress and duration anxiety are concentrating.

The structural explanation starts with liquidity and ends with fiscal credibility. Excess liquidity at **৳1.9 lakh crore** is abundant by any historical standard, and call money at **9.80%** — just 20 basis points below repo at **10%** — confirms that the interbank market is well-supplied but not flush enough to drive speculative demand into duration. Banks are sitting on cash, but with an **ADR of 75.8%** and credit growth evidently moderate, that cash is cycling into short-dated government paper rather than extending down the curve. The reason is not hard to find: CPI remains elevated at **9.13%**, which means real yields on the 91-day bill are barely positive, and any rational treasurer is demanding meaningful term premium to lock up capital for five years or more. The government's fiscal position amplifies this caution — revenue collection at just **44.9% of target** through Q3 of FY26, with ADP implementation at a dismal **11.5%**, signals that the fourth-quarter borrowing calendar will be heavy. Total debt at **৳21.5 lakh crore** consuming **22% of revenue** in interest alone means the market is rightly pricing a supply deluge as June approaches. The 2-year and 15-year kinks likely reflect specific issuance overhang or forced repricing at tenors where the government has been most active, and banks are simply refusing to absorb more without a concession. The flat zero-change week, in this context, is not calm — it is a standoff between a government that cannot afford to let yields rise further and a market that will not voluntarily extend duration at these levels.

For portfolio positioning this week, the tactical play is clear: stay short and selective. The **91-day at 9.85%** offers a **235 basis point** real yield over the SDF floor of 7.5% and rolls comfortably within the policy corridor — this is the cleanest carry in the market with no duration headache. The **364-day at 10.07%** is similarly attractive given its bid-cover strength and the near-certainty that BB holds repo at 10% through at least June. I would avoid the 2-year entirely — **11.95%** looks generous until you realize you are being paid to absorb issuance risk and potential mark-to-market pain if the government accelerates borrowing at that tenor into fiscal year-end. The genuine opportunity, for those with risk appetite, is the **5-year at 10.82%**: it cleared at **1.6x bid-cover with zero devolvement**, meaning the market found a clearing price it can live with, and at 82 basis points over repo it offers decent carry with a duration that does not extend into the next government's fiscal cycle. The 10-year remains a sell or underweight — that devolvement is an early warning, and if the next auction sees bid-cover slip below 1.3x, we could see BB forced into larger devolvement or an outright cutoff repricing, which would reprice the entire back end violently. The catalyst to watch is the April borrowing calendar: if the government front-loads issuance to compensate for the revenue shortfall, the belly and long end will break wider, and that frozen curve will thaw in the worst possible way. Conversely, if BB signals any willingness to trim repo by 25 basis points — unlikely given **9.13% CPI** but not impossible if food prices soften — the front end rallies and the curve begins to normalize. Until then, keep your powder dry and your duration short.
